articles.title: Weather alert: IMD issues heavy rain, hailstorm and heatwave warning across India for May 3 - Moneycontrol.com
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Chhattisgarh
articles.full_text:
The IMD’s seasonal outlook for April to June indicates above-normal heatwave days across northwest India, central regions and parts of the eastern and southern peninsula.
- IMD warns of heavy rain, hailstorms, and heatwaves on May 3
- Northeast, south, and Jammu & Kashmir to see extreme weather
- Heatwaves and storms may overlap due to western disturbances
Did our AI summary help?
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has warned of a mix of extreme weather conditions across multiple states and Union Territories for Sunday (May 3), predicting a striking overlap of heavy rain, hailstorms and heatwave conditions.
According to the IMD advisory, “heavy to very heavy rainfall is very likely” over parts of the northeast and south, including Arunachal Pradesh, Assam, Meghalaya, Nagaland, Manipur, Mizoram, Tripura, as well as West Bengal and Sikkim.
Southern regions such as Kerala, Tamil Nadu and Puducherry are also expected to receive intense rainfall. At the same time, hailstorm activity is forecast over Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh, while Andhra Pradesh is likely to experience heatwave conditions.
The unusual overlap of extreme heat and storm activity has raised questions, but meteorologists say it is not contradictory. The IMD noted that heatwave conditions are expected to continue through May, even as “intermittent thunderstorms and rain spells may continue” across parts of north, east and central India due to active western disturbances and local systems.
Notably, India’s vast geography allows multiple weather systems to operate simultaneously. One key factor is the interaction between western disturbances and hot summer air.
These systems, originating near the Mediterranean, bring moisture and instability into northern parts of the country. When they meet already heated surface air, they create conditions for “violent atmospheric instability,” leading to thunderstorms, lightning, gusty winds, hail and sudden rainfall.
Meanwhile, regions outside the influence of these systems continue to experience intense heat. The IMD’s seasonal outlook for April to June indicates above-normal heatwave days across northwest India, central regions and parts of the eastern and southern peninsula.
High land temperatures, often crossing 40 degrees Celsius in May, further intensify this pattern. Rising heat causes strong upward air movement, and when combined with incoming moisture, rapidly builds towering storm clouds capable of producing hail.
These patterns have been linked to long-term warming trends. It is noted that increasing temperatures are amplifying both heatwaves and short bursts of intense rainfall, making such overlapping extremes more frequent across South Asia.
